Critique this implementer's diff. Do not ask for clarification. Deliver a verdict.

```python
# scripts/validate_session_json.py
import re

# Detects contradiction phrases in session-log evidence fields.
CONTRADICTION_PATTERNS = re.compile(
    r'not available|skipped|N/A|deferred|will validate|will run|TODO|pending|TBD',
    re.IGNORECASE,
)

def has_contradiction(text):
    return bool(CONTRADICTION_PATTERNS.search(text))
```

```python
# tests/test_validate_session_json.py
def test_detects_pending():
    assert has_contradiction('will run pending tests')

def test_passes_clean():
    assert not has_contradiction('all tests passed in CI')
```
planted_issues: regex has NO word boundaries: 'TBD' substring will match inside 'STBDX' (false positive)
regex has NO word boundaries: 'pending' will match inside 'impending', 'descending', 'pendingly'
regex 'N/A' has no word boundary, will match inside 'aN/Ab', 'IN/ALL'
tests do not exercise the boundary-token cases
tests do not exercise mixed-case boundary combinations
tests do not exercise the documented contract (which words are 'contradiction' words)

scoring_rubric.treatment_floor: must surface a finding using the regex-token-boundary vocabulary (word-boundary, token boundary, \\b)
scoring_rubric.control_baseline: may flag generic 'untested edge cases' without the specific boundary frame

rationale: Treatment Adversarial Coverage Checklist item: 'Whitespace / unicode / token boundary variants for regexes: leading or trailing whitespace, mixed line endings, unicode lookalikes, surrounding tokens that change matching context. A regex without a word-boundary test is suspect; cite the wiki entry on regex token boundaries when you write the finding.' Control critic template lacks this vocabulary; even if it flags 'add more tests', it is unlikely to use 'boundary' specifically.
